coredata.dc:title: Boosting light harvesting of quaternary polymer solar cells through co-donor and co-acceptor with amine-functionalized graphene quantum dots and a ruthenium-based sensitizer
coredata.prism:publicationName: Colloids and Surfaces A: Physicochemical and Engineering Aspects
coredata.dc:creator: Abdollahi-Esfahlani, Hossein
Pourmahdian, Saeed
Ameri, Mohsen
coredata.dc:description: Currently, the quaternary polymer solar cells (PSCs) stand out as one of the most promising strategies for sustainable energy harvesting. However, it is challenging to successfully achieve efficient PSCs with a broad light absorption window and effective charge transport via introducing third and fourth components into the binary systems, while minimizing morphological heterogeneity, charge recombination, and cost of active materials. In this work, the amine-functionalized graphene quantum dots (NGQDs) with outstanding properties such as quantum confinement and doping ability are synthesized and characterized as the ternary component in PSCs. We report that the immobilization of tetrabutylammonium cis-bis(isothiocyanate)bis(2,2′-bipyridyl-4,4′-dicaboxylato) ruthenium (II) (N719) dye onto the surface of NGQDs and incorporation of them into the poly(3-hexylthiophene) (P3HT) and [6,6]-phenyl-C71-butyric acid methyl ester (PCBM) (P3HT:PCBM) system can promote the charge generation and Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET) between the photoactive materials. The photovoltaic results show more than 40 % improvement in power conversion efficiency (PCE) and a short circuit current (JSC) of 11.62 mA.cm−2 with only 0.25 wt% of co-sensitizers for the P3HT:PCBM:NGQD:N719 photovoltaic devices. The AFM results indicate that the quaternary modulation can improve the surface nanomorphology of bulk heterojunction towards reduced charge carrier recombination. The outcomes demonstrate a significant improvement in light harvesting and photovoltaic performance of the bulk heterojunction PSCs. These advancements contribute to the development of high-efficiency single-junction polymer solar cells.

head.ce:title: Boosting light harvesting of quaternary polymer solar cells through co-donor and co-acceptor with amine-functionalized graphene quantum dots and a ruthenium-based sensitizer

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Currently, the quaternary polymer solar cells (PSCs) stand out as one of the most promising strategies for sustainable energy harvesting. However, it is challenging to successfully achieve efficient PSCs with a broad light absorption window and effective charge transport via introducing third and fourth components into the binary systems, while minimizing morphological heterogeneity, charge recombination, and cost of active materials. In this work, the amine-functionalized graphene quantum dots (NGQDs) with outstanding properties such as quantum confinement and doping ability are synthesized and characterized as the ternary component in PSCs. We report that the immobilization of tetrabutylammonium cis-bis(isothiocyanate)bis(2,2′-bipyridyl-4,4′-dicaboxylato) ruthenium (II) (N719) dye onto the surface of NGQDs and incorporation of them into the poly(3-hexylthiophene) (P3HT) and [6,6]-phenyl-C71-butyric acid methyl ester (PCBM) (P3HT:PCBM) system can promote the charge generation and Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET) between the photoactive materials. The photovoltaic results show more than 40 % improvement in power conversion efficiency (PCE) and a short circuit current (JSC) of 11.62 mA.cm−2 with only 0.25 wt% of co-sensitizers for the P3HT:PCBM:NGQD:N719 photovoltaic devices. The AFM results indicate that the quaternary modulation can improve the surface nanomorphology of bulk heterojunction towards reduced charge carrier recombination. The outcomes demonstrate a significant improvement in light harvesting and photovoltaic performance of the bulk heterojunction PSCs. These advancements contribute to the development of high-efficiency single-junction polymer solar cells.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Nowadays, the energy crisis caused by fossil fuels has caused much more attention to be paid to energy harvesting from renewable and clean sources [1,2]. One of the most attractive technologies of green electricity generation is the bulk heterojunction (BHJ) polymer solar cells (PSCs), owing to their promising potential for solar energy harvesting with ease of roll-to-roll fabrication, flexibility, transparency, and high power-to-cost ratio [3–7]. In the BHJ structures, the photoactive layer is a nanoscale bi-continuous interpenetrating polymer network (IPN) of electron donor and electron acceptor materials, facilitating exciton dissociation to free charges [8,9]. The BHJ structure maximize the interface area between the donor and acceptor phases and provide continuous pathways for promoted hole and electron transport to the respective electrodes [10]. Many factors, including the nanomorphology of the BHJ layer, the chemical structure, and the composition of photoactive materials in the conducting inks, can influence the photovoltaic performance of PSCs [11–13].

Among the wide variety of photoactive materials used in the PSCs, a bench-mark p-type conjugated polymer, regio-regular poly(3-hexylthiophene) (P3HT), as the donor component and fullerene-based material, [6,6]-phenyl C71 butyric acid methyl ester (PCBM), as acceptor component are commonly blended in the solution systems [14–16]. Today, the power conversion efficiency (PCE) of polymer:fullerene PSCs has reached over 10 % in single-junction cells [17]. However, compared to the conventional silicon-based and perovskite photovoltaic devices, the BHJ PSCs have relatively low open circuit voltage (VOC) and low PCE, due to insufficient light harvesting in the photoactive layer with its narrow photon absorption window [18]. Moreover, a large energy offset between the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) levels of polymer donor and fullerene-based acceptor leads to high energy loss [19,20].

To overcome these limitations, many research efforts have been made to develop low band-gap (Eg) small-molecule and polymer semiconductors with broad absorption areas with respect to the solar spectrum [21,22]. However, this strategy requires precise synthetic and processing considerations. Another strategy to extend the photon absorption profile of the photoactive layer is designing multi-junction or tandem solar cells, which can reduce thermalization energy loss by absorption of long and short wavelength photons in each subcell [23–25]. Unfortunately, the processing and characterization challenges has dramatically lagged the transition from laboratory to commercial scale [24].

Alternatively, a simple and efficient approach for improving the photovoltaic performance of PSCs is the addition of ternary and quaternary components, such as dyes, quantum dots, nanoparticles, and conjugated polymers into the binary photoactive systems [26–29]. These components can act as either guest donor or acceptor materials for enhanced light harvesting, efficient exciton generation, and promoted charge or energy transport via the cascade-like energy levels and improved nanomorphology in the ternary BHJ systems [20]. The conjugated polymers with broad absorption bands, such as D18-Cl, PTB7-Th, PBB1-Cl, and PBBF have been incorporated into the binary systems comprised of polymers, such as PM6 and P3HT as donor components and fullerene derivatives or non-fullerene small molecules as acceptor components [30–32]. It should be noted that the performance of multicomponent PSCs can be affected severely by the photoactive ink formulation, the optical properties of conjugated components, and the morphology of materials in the BHJ [18,33].

Among the various ternary materials, the graphene quantum dots (GQDs) have attracted more attention in the fabrication of efficient ternary PSCs, because of their unique properties such as high charge mobility, large surface area, and tunable electronic and optical properties [34–36]. As a main processing problem, GQDs have large sizes (compared to the other types of photoactive materials) and a high tendency to aggregate in non-polymer domains, limiting their benefits as ternary components [36,37]. The incorporation of high contents of GQDs into the binary systems makes it difficult to form a uniform thin film. Moreover, GQDs have a narrow absorption band in the ultraviolet (UV) region (200–400 nm) [38,39]. Therefore, broadening the absorption band of GQD-containing BHJ layers can be an essential way to improve the light harvesting and Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET) between the GQDs and co-donor material in the multicomponent PSCs.

Several studies have reported the utilization of organic and organometal dyes, such as difluoroboron dipyrromethene (BODIPY) derivatives [40], squaraines (SQ) [41,42], thiophene derivatives [43], phthalocyanines [44,45], and rhodamine B [46] as the ternary and quaternary component in the PSCs. The di-tetrabutylammonium cis-bis(isothiocyanato)bis(2,2′-bipyridyl-4,4′-dicaboxylato) ruthenium (II), denoted as N719, is one of the simple ruthenium-based sensitizers, which commonly used in the dye-sensitized solar cells (DSSCs) [47,48]. These organometal dyes possesses high chemical and light stability, high absorption coefficient, and good solubility in common solvents [49,50]. Despite the recent developments of metal-free organic dyes, the state-of-art N719 molecules, still exhibit a relatively broad absorption band (∼350 nm bandwidth) and rapid charge transfer between the electron-rich and electron-poor molecular sections [49,51].

Nevertheless, these approaches usually suffer from their high cost, morphology heterogeneity, and incorporation of high concentration additives. As a novel approach, it is expected that the sensitization of GQDs with N719 dyes, can broaden the absorption window of the P3HT:PCBM-based quaternary BHJs in the visible region. Therefore, N719-sensitized GQDs can promote light harvesting and charge transfer in the ternary PSCs by introducing efficient transfer channels. Generally, Dimitriev and coworkers demonstrated that the interaction between the tricarbocyanine dyes and GQDs leads to the immobilization of dye molecules and a significant enhancement of the emission, which is known as “awakening”, from Sn (n ˃ 2) energy levels (anti-Kasha emission) in the visible region [52]. It is believed that this phenomenon can also improve the photovoltaic performance of ternary PSCs with low content of GQDs via promoted FRET between the N719-sensitized GQDs and P3HT [53]. Despite of the extensive investigation on ternary and quaternary modulation in PSCs, incorporation of a GQD-N719 couple with their dual effect on light-harvesting and efficient energy transfer have been not still studied.

Herein, we present a novel approach where amine-functionalized GQDs are pre-sensitized with the N719 dyes (N719-NGQDs), and their incorporation into the binary P3HT:PCBM system is systematically studied for the first time. This unique N719-NGQD system, with its multiple components, shows promising potential for efficient quaternary PSCs. Our research breaks new ground as it is the first to report the sensitization of amine-functionalized GQDs with organometal dyes and their application in the quaternary PSCs. We demonstrate that the addition of N719 and NGQD co-sensitizers into the binary BHJ leads to improved light harvesting in a broad absorption window and efficient charge and energy transport. Furthermore, we show that the low contents of NGQD in combination with the N719 dye can enhance the thin-film nanomorphology and, subsequently, the PCE of the quaternary PSCs.

Citric acid (CA), ethylene diamine (EDA), Acetone, ethanol, 1,2-dichlorobenzene (DCB), and chlorobenzene (CB) were purchased from Merck Millipore. The di-tetrabutylammonium cis-bis(isothiocyanato)bis(2,2′-bipyridyl-4,4′-dicaboxylato) ruthenium (II) (N719, >97 % purity) as the co-sensitizer dye was supplied from Ossila. 1.1 wt% aqueous dispersion of poly (3,4-ethylene dioxythiophene):poly (styrene sulfonate) (PEDOT:PSS, clevios PH1000) was obtained from Heraeus. poly(3-hexylthiophene) (P3HT) (with a molecular weight of ∼45,000 g/mol and >93 % regioregularity) and [6,6]-phenyl C71 butyric acid methyl ester (PCBM, 99.5 % purity) as the photoactive components were purchased from Lumtec. All the chemicals were used as received without any purification.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of amine-functionalized graphene quantum dots (NGQDs)
ce:section.ce:para: NGQDs were synthesized according to a hydrothermal modified method reported in the previous works [39,54]. In this procedure, CA (2.101 g) and EDA (670 μL) were dissolved in 30 mL deionized (DI) water as the GQD precursor and nitrogen heteroatom doping agent, respectively. Then, the transparent solution was transferred to the 50 mL poly(tetrafluoroethylene) (Teflon)-lined stainless-steel autoclave followed by heating at 150 °C for 5 h. The reactor was left to cool to room temperature naturally. Afterward, the dark brown solution was filtered using the dialysis tube with a pore size of 3.5 kDa (Spectra/Por 3 RC) against the DI water for 3–5 days to obtain the NGQDs with the smallest sizes. To remove any impurities and unreacted residues, the filtrate solution was subjected to the dialysis tube with a pore size of 1 kDa (Spectra/Por 7 RC) for 2 days. Finally, the brownish solid was obtained after freeze-drying for 2 days to utilize for further characterization and applications. Fig. 1 shows the schematic illustration of the one-pot synthesis of NGQDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Pre-sensitization of NGQDs with N719 dyes
ce:section.ce:para: In order to improve the optical properties of both the NGQDs and N719 dyes, the NGQDs were sensitized with the N719 via the interaction between carboxylic groups of N719 and the amine (NH2) groups of NGQDs [35]. For this purpose, 3 mL (10 mg/mL) of NGQD aqueous dispersion was prepared by ultrasonication for 15 min at room temperature. Then, two amounts (8 and 16 mg) of N719 dye were added to the solution, stirring at 110 °C to ensure completed N719-functionalization of NQDs. After 48 h, the resultant solutions were dialyzed with the 1 kDa tubing bag for 48 h to remove any unreacted dyes. The final two N719-NGQD powders (N719-NGQD 1 and N719-NGQD 2) were obtained by freeze-drying the residue solution for 2 days.

Prepatterned glass/ITO substrates were washed in the hot detergent solution and then sonicated in DI water, acetone, and isopropanol for 15 min, respectively. The substrates were dried by highly pure nitrogen (N2) gas and subsequently treated under UV/ozone for 15 min. The treatment step helps to enhance the surface hydrophilicity. The hole transport layer (HTL) was deposited on the glass/ITO substrates via spin-coating the PEDOT:PSS solution (150 μL) at 3000 rpm for 30 s, followed by thermal annealing at 120 °C for 15 min on a digital hot plate. The HTL thickness was measured ca. 30 nm by profilometry. The binary P3HT:PCBM active blends were dissolved (with a total concentration of 18 mg/mL) in DCB with 1:1 ratio by weight. The solution was stirred at 60 °C for overnight. The conducting ink (100 μL) was spin-coated onto the HTL at 1500 rpm for 30 s in the ambient conditions. P3HT:PCBM as-prepared thin films underwent two steps of post-treatment: (i) solvent vapor annealing for 10 min under CB vapor in a covered petri dish, and (ii) thermal annealing at 150 °C for 10 min [55].

To prepare the ternary blends, 0.1 wt% (relative to P3HT) NGQDs were dispersed in 1 mL of DCB by ultrasonication for 15 min at room temperature. Then, 0.5 mL of as-prepared binary P3HT:PCBM solution was mixed with 0.5 mL of NGQD dispersion to obtain a 1:1:0.001 ternary P3HT:PCBM:NGQD solution, followed by stirring for about 2 h at 60 °C. The blend solution (100 μL) was spin-coated atop the HTL at 1500 rpm for 30 min. The above-mentioned post-treatment steps were also applied to ternary photoactive thin films. The semi-quaternary blends were prepared by mixing two concentrations of N719-NGQD dispersions with the binary blends to obtain a 1:1:0.001 and 1:1:0.002 ratio by weight for P3HT:PCBM:NGQD:N719 1 and P3HT:PCBM:NGQD:N719 2 solutions. These solutions were also spin-coated on the top of the PEDOT:PSS layer under the same environmental conditions and post-treatment processes.

Finally, a thin layer of Al (ca. 100 nm) was thermally evaporated on the photoactive layers using the physical vapor deposition (PVD) under a vacuum of ≤ 1 × 10−6 mbar. The device area was measured ca. 0.13 cm2, which was defined by the crossing area of patterned ITO and Al cathode.

The chemical structures of NGQDs and their possible interactions of them with N719 were analyzed using the Fourier transform infrared (FTIR; Bruker Tensor 27) spectroscopy in the wavenumber range of 4000–500 cm−1 at a resolution of 4 cm−1 in the absorption mode. The images obtained from high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM; FEI TECNAI F20) at 200 kV were applied to investigate the surface morphology and elemental analysis of NGQD. Moreover, the size and thickness of NGQD were calculated by analyzing the HRTEM images. The X-ray diffraction (XRD; Inel EQUINOX3000) pattern for NGQD was recorded with Cu Kα radiation (λ = 1.5418 Å) at a generation voltage of 40 kV and a current of 30 mA. UV–vis spectroscopy (Perkin Elmer Lambda 45) was utilized for studying the absorption properties of solutions and dried thin films at the wavelength range of 200–800 nm and a scan rate of 1 nm. The photoluminescence (PL) characteristics of the samples were investigated using the PL spectrometer (Perkin Elmer LS 55). The excitation wavelength (λexc = 360 nm) was selected for the samples. To study the BHJ morphology in the binary, ternary, and quaternary thin films the topography and phase images obtained by atomic force microscope (AFM, ARA Nanoscope) were analyzed in contact mode at a 1.5 × 1.5 cm2 surface area.

The final devices' photovoltaic characteristics were obtained using a current-voltage (I-V) source meter unit (Keithley 2400) under AM 1.5G (100 mW.cm−2) illumination by a 150 W solar simulator (IRASOL UPT-100). A standard Si solar cell calibrated the illumination intensity. All of the I-V measurements were carried out in the air and at room temperature.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Structural analysis of NGQD and N719-NGQD
ce:section.ce:para: Hydrothermal synthesis of GQDs is a successful technique due to its simplicity and capability for doping the various heteroatoms in the GQD structure [38]. According to the literature, a 1:1 molar ratio was selected for CA:EDA precursors to achieve high photoluminescence quantum yield (PLQY) [39]. As illustrated in Fig. 2, a semi-polymerization reaction occurred between the trifunctional CA with difunctional EDA to form the polymer-like quantum nanoparticles. In the next step, the NGQDs are formed under the carbonization of polymer-like nanoparticles [56]. In this process, the presence of EDA leads to obtaining the GQDs with amine (-NH2) functional groups. Amine-functionalization was performed to efficiently interact with the carboxyl-containing N719 dye molecules and the NGQDs.

High-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM) images of NGQDs show an average size of about 5.24 nm ( Fig. 3a) with a quite uniform distribution for particle size between 2.75 and 7.25 nm (Fig. 3b). Through the high-magnification images of HR-TEM, it is observed that the lattice spacing was 0.257 nm. The results are in good accordance with the size values reported in the literature [57]. The EDS analysis results are listed in Table 1. The elemental study shows the success of functionalization during the one-pot synthesis of NGQDs.

The structural and crystalline properties of NGQDs were analyzed by XRD patterns (Fig. 3d). The NGQDs comprise a set of sp2 carbons with edge functionalities, which show a broad 002 band at about 2θ = 25.98°. This amorphous peak is related to the highly disordered carbon atoms, indicating that the calculated interlayer distance was 3.42 Å. This value is larger than the interlayer distance between the graphene layers of bulk graphite (∼ 3.4 Å), which can be related to the repulsive forces due to the edge sites [58]. The presence of functional groups in the edge states reduces the π-π stacking of graphene layers and subsequently broadens the amorphous peak in the XRD spectra [39]. Fig. S1 presents the (100) plane FFT pattern for NGQDs with hexagonal structure in the [0001] direction.

The chemical structure of NGQD as well as the formation of imide bonds in the N719-NGQD were investigated by the FTIR analysis (Fig. 3e). Based on the FTIR spectra, the NGQD shows a broad absorption peak at about 3420 cm−1, which is related to the -OH and -NH2 on the surfaces of quantum dots [59,60]. Moreover, the characteristic peaks of C-H can be seen approximately at 2970 and 2910 cm−1 [56,60]. The absorption band at about 1770 and 1700 cm−1 can be attributed to the stretching vibrations of CO in the NGQD structure [61]. Meanwhile, the presence of characteristic peaks at about 1587, 1361, and 1159 cm−1, correspond to amine N-H vibrations, aromatic C-N, and stretching vibrations of amine C-N, respectively [56]. These peaks confirm the amine-functionalization of GQDs. The main characteristic peaks of N719 dye are presence at about 2105, 1718, 1540, 1471, and 1234 cm−1 for thiocyanate groups (NCS), CO, CC, tetrabutylammonium (TBA) counterions, and carboxylic C-O groups, respectively [62,63]. Moreover, the absorption band at 1608 and 1363 cm−1 can attributed to the asymmetric and symmetric vibrations of COO- groups, respectively [63]. By overlaying, it can be seen that the absorption peak located approximately at 3400 cm−1 is broadened and the shoulder at about 3228 cm−1 (N-H bond) is also shifted to slightly higher energy (∼ 6 cm−1), which may be due to linking the N719 to the NGQD surface [53]. Moreover, the shifts in the CO absorption band position compared to the N719 dye (from 1718 cm−1 to 1704 cm−1) also confirm the amide bond formation [64]. The absorption band related to aromatic C-N bond shifts from 1159 to 1171 cm−1 is also evidence for generating the new covalent bond. It should be noted that the significant reduction of the NCS absorption band at about 2105 cm−1 can be related to the anchoring mechanism of dye to graphene surfaces of NGQDs [62].

The UV–vis spectrum ( Fig. 4a) of NGQD in water shows two absorption bands at around 240 and 340 nm, which are related to electronic π-π* and n-π* transitions, respectively [39,57]. The strong peak at low wavelength demonstrated that the conjugated sp2 structure of NGQDs is still preserved even after the functionalization with amine and carboxyl groups [65]. Meanwhile, the presence of an absorption band at the high wavelength belongs to surface functional groups and doping of nitrogen heteroatoms at the edges [61]. The weak absorption tail at the visible region is attributed to the edge states, which can help to efficient sunlight harvesting [61]. The N719 dye solution shows four intense bands in the UV–vis spectrum. The strong peaks at 245 and 309 nm (UV region) are assigned to the intraligand (π-π*) electronic transitions [63,66]. Also, the broad bands at 370 and 500 nm are related to the metal-to-ligand charge transfer (MLTC) transitions from dπ orbitals of Ru (II) to π* orbitals of bipyridine carboxylate groups [67]. The chemical sensitization of NGQDs with two different amounts of N719 dye caused the immediate growth of overall absorption below the wavelength of ∼ 420 nm. Moreover, the S0 → S1 transition faded in the visible region compared to the N719 absorption band even after the purification of N719-NGQD solutions. These behaviors may be due to the interaction between the NGQD and N719 by the immobilizing of dye molecules onto the graphene surfaces [52]. Adding a high amount of N719 dye to the NGQD structure intensely enhanced the absorption bands, which can arise from the aggregation of dye molecules onto the quantum dot.

Improving of optical properties via the sensitization of NGQDs with dye can enhance light harvesting in optoelectronic applications. Fig. 4b shows the PL emission spectra of pure sensitizers, N719-NGQD 1, and N719-NGQD 2 solutions. It can be seen that the S2 emission of dye molecules awakened due to the suppression of distorted conformations of dye molecules and their freezing onto the NGQD surface. However, a high amount of dye sensitizers reduced the PL intensity. On the other hand, the S1 emission is fully suppressed under the N719-sensitization of NGQDs. Therefore, the radiative recombination of charges weakened in the N719-NGQDs. Fig. S2 shows the visual PL of the NGQD dispersion under the sunlight excitation.

Fig. 5a shows the absorption spectra of P3HT and P3HT:PCBM binary solution in DCB. For the P3HT solution, a broad absorption peak was observed in the wavelength range of about 300–550 nm, which is related to the energetic transition of conjugated structure (π-π*) [46]. Although, the P3HT absorption band is a suitable accordance with the solar irradiation spectrum, but incorporation of the second donor with complementary absorption has a positively enhances the absorption window and light harvesting. The P3HT:PCBM solution exhibits two main peaks at around 330 and 467 nm, which are attributed to the π-π* transitions and intramolecular charge transfer between the photoactive materials, respectively [50]. Fig. 5b displays the optical absorption of binary, ternary, and semi-quaternary photoactive thin films. As can be seen, the P3HT:PCBM binary film exhibits a wide absorption window with one peak centered at around 498 nm and two main shoulders approximately at 532 and 586 nm, which are related to the P3HT donor material [68]. Moreover, the absorption band at about 376 nm is related to the PCBM acceptor material. By adding the low concentration of NGQDs into the binary solution (keeping a constant P3HT:PCBM weight ratio of 1:1), it can be observed that the absorption intensity increases in the UV region (< 400 nm). However, the absorption of NGQD-incorporated ternary thin films dramatically reduces in the visible region due to the very weak absorption band of NGQDs in the wavelengths up to 400 nm. The more intensive and red-shifted absorption band for PCBM peak in the ternary and semi-quaternary thin films is related to the intensive intramolecular interactions between the PCBM, NGQD, and N719 dyes, which can be ascribed to the formation of PCBM:NGQD:N719 miscible blend because of their polar nature [36,46]. Upon incorporating of the N719:NGQDs, the broadened and intensive absorption spectra (between 350 and 750 nm) can be achieved for both semi-quaternary thin films, which will increase the capability of light harvesting in the PSCs. However, higher concentration of N719 dye in the P3HT:PCBM:N719:NGQD 2 thin film has fewer absorption spectra in the visible region. Moreover, it can be noted that N719 dye can act as the second donor material. The significant enhancement of 0–2, 0–1, and 0–0 absorption intensities of P3HT bands in the quaternary blend films may have led to stronger H- and J-aggregations, and subsequently improved charge mobility and reduced charge recombination [69].

Non-radiative FRET is an effective approach to enhance the photovoltaic properties of PSCs [70]. There are two main possible FRET effects in the P3HT:PCBM:NGQD:N719 quaternary system between the P3HT, NGQD, and N719 due to overlap the emission spectrum of NGQD with the absorption spectrum of the N719, as well as the emission spectrum of N719 with the absorption spectrum of the P3HT (Fig. 5c, d). In addition to the electron-donating nature of N719, it can act as the FRET agent. Therefore, the excited electron in the N719 molecules can be transferred to the NGQD and subsequently PCBM, or relaxed to emit sufficient energy for excitation of P3HT. A similar phenomenon can occur between the NGQD and N719. The FRET effect leads to the generation of excess excitons, which can enhance the charge density collected in the respective electrodes [71].

To examine the photovoltaic performance of PSCs based on different blends of photoactive materials, the binary, ternary, and quaternary solar cells with a standard configuration of ITO/PEDOT:PSS/photoactive layer/Al were fabricated. The schematic illustration of the final device is shown in Fig. 2. Moreover, Fig. 6 shows the J-V curves of four P3HT:PCBM-based devices with ternary and quaternary components. Also, the detailed photovoltaic parameters are summarized in Table 2. As can be seen, the control device based on P3HT:PCBM exhibits a VOC of 0.53 V, a short-circuit current (JSC) of 9.75 mA.cm−2 and a fill factor of 0.40, resulting in a PCE of 2.09 %. The photovoltaic values are lower than those reported in the literature because all fabrication steps were done at ambient conditions to mimic simple environmental conditions. Although, the incorporation of NGQDs into the P3HT:PCBM decreased the light absorption, but the PCE was improved to 2.38 % due to the increased JSC and FF. To investigate the effect of the quaternary component, N719 dye, on the photovoltaic performance, two concentrations of dye were added into the ternary P3HT:PCBM:NGQD system. The quaternary PSC with a low content of N719 and the most optimized PCE of 2.97 attained a JSC of 11.62 mA.cm−2 and FF of 0.48. The high content of dye additives has a negative effect on photovoltaic parameters by reducing the PCE from 2.98 % to 2.69 %. As a result, this suggests that the BHJ nanostructure between the P3HT and PCBM became disrupted at high concentration of N719 [46,50]. The photovoltaic results from the quaternary devices are in consistent with UV–vis data of the samples. Interestingly, the VOC values of all PSCs remain constant, which arise from the adjacency of HOMO levels of P3HT to NGQD and N719.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The effect of multiple components on the thin film topography and surface properties
ce:section.ce:para: It is proposed that thin film nanomorphology and surface topography can significantly affect the photovoltaic performance in ternary and quaternary PSCs [46,72]. The phase separation in the various BHJ layers was examined using atomic force microscopy (AFM, Fig. 7). As shown in the height images, the P3HT:PCBM:NGQD ternary system exhibits a root-mean-square roughness (Rq) of 1.69 nm (Fig. 7c), which is slightly larger than that of the binary system (Fig. 7a, Rq = 1.50 nm). The high tendency of NGQDs to aggregate along with their compatibility with PCBM acceptors can result in the promotion of molecular aggregation, forming large domains. Interestingly, when the quaternary component (N719 dye) was incorporated into the ternary system, the surface roughness decreased (Rq = 1.44 nm), which can be related to the change in the phase separation (Fig. 7e), consistent with previous findings reported in the literature [27]. Note that this phenomenon may be due to the modified intermolecular interactions between the active components [27]. To investigate the effect of N719 dye on the surface morphology of quaternary PSCs, the high content of dye molecule was added to the ternary system. As illustrated in the Fig. 7g, the surface roughness dramatically increased (Rq = 1.73 nm), due to the aggregation of dye molecules. Therefore, although the high concentration of N719 enhances the light-harvesting, the island-like domains with highly aggregated domains suppress the efficient exciton dissociation and charge transfer to the respective electrodes. Further information extracted from the phase images that the phase separation changes in the ternary and quaternary systems (Fig. 7b, d, f, and h). It can be seen that the incorporation of ternary and quaternary components into the binary system causes a reduction in the domain sizes while retaining the overall P3HT microfibrillar patterns. As organic semiconductors suffer from limited exciton diffusion length, achieving the nanoscale continuous phase separation has key importance for the enhancement of photovoltaic performance. Therefore, adding the NGQDs and N719 to the P3HT:PCBM synergistically improves the nanomorphology, and subsequently is a positive parameter for exciton separation and charge transfer.

Fig. S3, shows the water contact angle (WCA) of thin films. The results indicate that as we expect the surface hydrophilicity enhanced after incorporation of NGQDs and N719 dyes. The low difference between the WCA of P3HT:PCBM and P3HT:PCBM:NGQD thin films may be due to the capsulation of quantum dots into the PCBM domains. After adding the N719 to the ternary systems, it can be seen that the dye molecules incorporated onto the surface of the photoactive layer [73].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Charge transfer mechanism in the quaternary PSCs
ce:section.ce:para: As described in the previous research work by Sung et al., the GQDs are distributed in the PCBM-rich domains because of their polar nature [36]. On the other hand, N719 dye molecules are also polar compounds that tend to be present in the PCBM phase. Therefore, there are two main phases in the quaternary BHJ layer, P3HT-rich, and PCBM-rich, in which NGQDs and N719 molecules are occluded. The HOMO and LUMO levels of quaternary active materials play a crucial role in the prediction of final device performance [74]. The energy levels of the components of ternary and quaternary blend PSCs are depicted in Fig. 8. According to the energy level diagram, P3HT and N719 can act as the donor materials to harvest the solar energy and generate the excitons, followed by transferring the electrons to the NGQD and PCBM. In fact, the NGQD is a channel for efficient charge transfer by reducing energy loss [20,58]. Moreover, the FRET of NGQD-N719 and N719-P3HT couples (Fig. 5c, d) leads to the generation of more excitons. It should be noted that the FRET from the NGQDs to the P3HT is negligible, due to the long distance between them. The photoactive components exhibit cascade-like LUMO levels which is favorable for exciton dissociation and electron transfer to the Al cathode. Additionally, the appropriate alignment of HOMO levels can help to efficient hole transfer to the PEDOT:PSS layer.

Table 3 presents a comparison of key photovoltaic properties for quaternary PSCs developed in this research work with data from other PSCs reported in the literature. The results indicate that high JSC and PCE can be achieved by adding very low contents of ternary and quaternary active components. Meanwhile, a high PCE enhancement of 40 % was achieved for the best device compared to the control P3HT:PCBM-based device. However, the relatively low photovoltaic parameters when compared with the common values reported in the literature can be due to the fabrication of device in the ambient conditions and the lack of an ETL layer such as LiF.

In conclusion, the co-donor and co-acceptor concept was proposed to fabricate the quaternary photovoltaic devices using the synthesized amine-functionalized graphene quantum dots (NGQDs) via the bottom-up hydrothermal reactions and their pre-sensitization with N719 dyes. The chemical and photophysical characterizations show the promising potential of incorporating the NGQD and N719 as the ternary and quaternary components in the PSCs. By implementing very low amounts of NGQD and N719 into the P3HT:PCBM, approximately 40 % improvement of PCE accompanied with JSC of 11.62 mA cm−2 and a notably increased FF (0.48) can be achieved. Although the NGQDs were previously added to the P3HT:PCBM by several researchers in the current work the immobilization of N719 onto the graphene surface and strong FRET from this couple were investigated. Mainly, the N719 dyes act as both the co-sensitizers and FRET agents to the P3HT donor to enhance the light harvesting and density of photogenerated excitons. Moreover, we present that the quaternary modulation leads to the improvement of bi-continuous BHJ structures. Our findings emphasize that utilizing a multicomponent approach during the fabrication of PSCs can serve as a reliable strategy to enhance light absorption and minimize carrier recombination, leading to more efficient polymer photovoltaic devices.
